unit.title: Food And Everyday Preferences
unit.communicationGoal: Name common Kyrgyz foods, express a simple want or preference, accept or decline an offer politely, and respond to a host at a tea table.
pronunciationFocus: Жакшы көрөм — the ö in көрөм is a front rounded vowel, hold it clearly
Каалайм ends with a nasal м; do not drop it
Тамак has stress on the first syllable: TA-mak

lexemes.notes: The general word for food or a cooked meal. Тамак жеңиз means 'please eat' and is a standard host invitation. Тамак ичүү literally means eating-drinking and can refer to a meal event.
The round flatbread central to Kyrgyz meals. Нан is never placed on the floor or turned upside down — it carries cultural significance as a staple and sacred food.

Tea is central to Kyrgyz hospitality. Guests are offered чай almost immediately upon arriving. Accepting tea is polite; refusing can seem cold. Кара чай is plain black tea; ак чай (white tea) has milk.
The word for meat generally. Mutton and beef are most common. Эт a common part of many Kyrgyz dish names such as бешбармак (big five-finger dish with meat and noodles).
The general word for fruit. Apricots, apples, and melons are common in Kyrgyz markets. Мөмө жечи — would you like some fruit — is a warm host offer.
First-person singular present tense of каалоо (to want, to wish). In polite speech you can soften it by adding өтүнөм before it or by using the -чы suffix: Чай берчи (please give tea).
Literally 'I see/find it good'. The standard way to say you like something. Мен нан жакшы көрөм — I like bread. More positive than just каалайм; it expresses genuine liking.
The negated form of жактыруу. More direct than жакшы көрбөйм. Useful for politely declining food you genuinely dislike, but soften it with a smile and рахмат.
Used as acceptance to an offer. When someone offers you tea, болот is a friendly way to say yes please. Болбойт means no / it won't work, used for soft refusals.
Literally 'earth apple'. Potatoes are a staple in Kyrgyz cooking. The compound word mirrors French pomme de terre and German Erdapfel — a useful memory hook.
Dairy features strongly in Kyrgyz food culture. Сүт is used in tea, as a drink, and in many fermented products. The ü vowel is front rounded — round your lips.

sentences.translation: Thank you, I have had enough.
What would you like to eat?
You could give me tea with milk.

grammarNotes.title: каалайм and жакшы көрөм both express wanting
grammarNotes.summary: Kyrgyz has two common ways to say you want or like something. Каалайм (I want / I would like) is more about desire in the moment, like ordering food. Жакшы көрөм (I like) describes a general preference. Both follow the subject-object-verb order: Мен чай каалайм (I tea want = I want tea). Neither requires additional words to make a complete sentence.

grammarNotes.title: Polite offers use the -сызбы / -сизби question suffix
grammarNotes.summary: To offer something politely in Kyrgyz, take the polite verb form and add -бы or -би for a yes-no question. Чай ичесизби means 'would you like to drink tea?' The -сиз part marks polite register and -би turns it into a question. You can answer simply болот (okay/yes) or рахмат, жок (thank you, no).

grammarNotes.title: Adjectives precede nouns without changing form
grammarNotes.summary: In Kyrgyz, adjectives come before the noun they describe and do not take case suffixes. Сүттүү чай means 'milky tea' or 'tea with milk' — сүттүү (milky) sits directly before чай (tea). This is one of the most beginner-friendly patterns in the language: there is no agreement in gender, number, or case on the adjective itself.

tasks.objective: Respond to a host's food and drink offers using the phrase bank from this unit.
tasks.instructions: You are a guest at a Kyrgyz home. Your host will make three offers: tea, bread, and a main dish. For each offer, respond using a phrase from the bank. Accept at least two things and politely decline one.
successCriteria: Accept a drink offer using болот or ооба, рахмат.
Express a food preference using жакшы көрөм or каалайм.
Decline one offer politely using рахмат, жок or жактырбайм.
Thank the host at the end using рахмат or чоң рахмат.
tasks.completionHint: You can always say 'Рахмат, мен токтодум' to politely stop eating or decline more food.

supportCards.title: Tea is a social ritual in Kyrgyz homes
supportCards.body: Refusing tea from a host can feel unwelcoming. If you are not thirsty, accept the cup, hold it warmly, and take small sips. The gesture of receiving matters more than the amount you drink.

supportCards.title: Bolot vs bolboyt
supportCards.body: Болот means it's okay / yes please when accepting an offer. Болбойт (with the negation -бой-) means it won't work / no, and is a gentle soft refusal. Both are polite.

tasks.scenario: You have been invited to a family's home for a meal. The host is generous and will offer you several things. Use your phrase bank to respond naturally.

reviewSeeds.prompt: How do you say 'tea' in Kyrgyz?
What does 'Мен нан жакшы көрөм' mean?
How do you say 'I would like meat' in Kyrgyz?
What does 'жер алма' literally mean, and what food is it?
